title: Shaped paper forms
subtitle: Casting wet pulp over and into shaped moulds to make three-dimensional paper bowls, vessels, and sculptural objects
excerpt: Pulp casting takes the same beaten cotton fibre used for flat sheets and presses it directly onto a mould. As the water drains and the pulp dries, it takes the exact shape of the surface it was cast onto. The dried casting is light, rigid, and takes paint, ink, and collage additions. Simple bowls, trays, and relief tiles are achievable with kitchen equipment and cotton-linter pulp.
sourceNotes: Sylvia Hurst, Paper Art: The Complete Guide to Papermaking and the Creation of New Art Forms (1993). Out of copyright in most jurisdictions and widely reproduced. Hurst introduced pulp casting to a wide Western crafting audience.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of pressing or pouring wet paper pulp into or over a shaped mould to create a three-dimensional paper form. Unlike flat sheet-pulling, the pulp is applied by hand in a thick layer and builds up by addition rather than suspension.

Pulp prepared at a higher fibre concentration than sheet-pulling slurry, typically 3-5% fibre by weight versus the 0.5-1% used for flat sheets. Denser pulp holds its shape during application and produces a stronger finished casting.

Waiting for a paper casting to dry completely before attempting to separate it from the mould. Releasing a damp casting tears the fibres because the wet paper has very low wet strength. The casting is ready to release when it feels rigid and sounds hollow when tapped.

content.text: bypasses the mould-and-deckle: pulp is pressed onto a shaped surface by hand, built up in an even layer, and left to dry onto the form. The pulp has no grain direction and bonds to itself as it dries, so the finished piece picks up both the surface texture and the overall shape.
Prepare cotton-linter pulp and drain most of the water to make

: the blended pulp should feel like thick yoghurt, not water with floating fibres. Too thin and it runs off vertical surfaces; too thick and it dries with cracks. If the pulp holds its shape when pressed into a clump in the hand, the concentration is right.

Before applying pulp, coat the mould surface with a release agent: petroleum jelly, vegetable oil, or diluted washing-up liquid all work. This prevents the casting bonding to the mould during drying. Press small handfuls of pulp onto the surface, working outward from the centre, and compress each handful firmly with the sponge. Build to a thickness of 5-8 mm for a sturdy bowl.

Leave the casting on the mould in a warm place for 24-48 hours. Do not attempt to hurry the drying with a heat gun, this shrinks the outer surface faster than the inner, causing warping. The casting is ready for
when it sounds hollow when tapped and the edges are curling slightly away from the mould. Peel gently from one edge.

items.symptom: The casting tears when lifted from the mould
items.cause: Released too early while still damp, or the release agent was not applied.
items.fix: Wait until the casting sounds hollow all over before releasing. Apply petroleum jelly to the mould before the next cast.

items.symptom: The finished bowl has uneven thickness, thin spots and thick ridges
items.cause: The pulp was applied in large handfuls rather than small distributed amounts.
items.fix: Apply the pulp in pieces no larger than a 50p coin. Check thickness with a finger after each section and level unevenness by pressing rather than adding more pulp.
